(a) $\Lambda / \Lambda^{\prime} / \Lambda^{\prime \prime} / \cdots$. (b) $Z / 2 Z / \boldsymbol{4} \boldsymbol{Z} / \boldsymbol{8} \boldsymbol{Z} / \cdots$. nary representation is illustrated in this way in Fig. 5(b) (note that the "least significant bit" $a_{0}$ appears at the top). ## C. Geometric Properties The geometry of a real lattice $\Lambda$ arises from the geometry of a real Euclidean $N$-space $\boldsymbol{R}^{N}$. The two principal geometrical parameters of $\Lambda$ are the minimum squared distance $d_{\min }^{2}(\Lambda)$ between its points and its fundamental volume $V(\Lambda)$; these determine its fundamental coding gain $\gamma(\Lambda)$. The norm $\|\boldsymbol{x}\|^{2}$ of a vector $\boldsymbol{x}$ in $\boldsymbol{R}^{N}$ is the sum of the squares of its coordinates. Norms are nonnegative and in fact nonzero unless $\boldsymbol{x}=\mathbf{0}$. The squared distance between two vectors $\boldsymbol{x}$ and $\boldsymbol{y}$ is the norm of their difference $\|x-y\|^{2}$. Because a lattice $\Lambda$ consists of discrete points, the norms of all lattice points are an infinite set of discrete values that can be enumerated in ascending order We call this the weight distribution of the lattice (theta series, in the lattice literature). The weight distribution is also the squared distance distribution between any point in the lattice and all other points, since any point $\lambda$ in $\Lambda$ can be taken as the origin $\mathbf{0}$ by translation of $\Lambda$ by $\lambda$ (looking out from any point in $\Lambda$, the lattice looks the same). The minimum nonzero norm is thus the minimum squared distance $d_{\min }^{2}(\Lambda)$ between any two points in $\Lambda$. The number of elements of $\Lambda$ with this norm is the number of nearest neighbors of any lattice point (also called the kissing number, or multiplicity), and will be called here the error coefficient $N_{0}(\Lambda)$. For example, for any $N$, the integer lattice $\boldsymbol{Z}^{N}$ has $d_{\text {min }}^{2}\left(\boldsymbol{Z}^{N}\right)=1$. The set of all integer $N$-tuples of norm 1 is the set of all permutations and sign changes of the vector $(1,0, \cdots, 0)$, so $N_{0}\left(\boldsymbol{Z}^{N}\right)=2 N$. Loosely, the fundamental volume $V(\Lambda)$ is the volume of $N$-space per lattice point, or the reciprocal of the number of lattice points per unit volume. More precisely, if we can partition $N$-space into regions of equal volume, one associated with each lattice point, then $V(\Lambda)$ is the volume of each such region. For example, it is easy to see that we may partition $N$-space into $N$-cubes of side 1, one associated with each point of $\boldsymbol{Z}^{N}$, so $V\left(\boldsymbol{Z}^{N}\right)=1$. To treat the general case, note that $\boldsymbol{R}^{N}$ is itself a group under ordinary vector addition (but not a lattice), because its points are not discrete). Any real $N$-dimensional lattice $\Lambda$ is a subgroup of $\boldsymbol{R}^{N}$. Thus there is a partition $\boldsymbol{R}^{N} / \Lambda$ of $N$-space into equivalence classes modulo $\Lambda$ (cosets of $\Lambda$ ) (in our original definition of a coset of $\Lambda$, implicitly we meant a coset in the partition $\boldsymbol{R}^{N} / \Lambda$ ). Define a fundamental region $\mathbb{R}(\Lambda)$ as a region of $N$-space that contains one and only one point from each such equivalence class modulo $\Lambda$; thus $\mathbb{R}(\Lambda)$ is a system of coset representatives for the partition $\boldsymbol{R}^{N} / \Lambda$. Every point $\boldsymbol{x}$ in $\boldsymbol{R}^{N}$ is thus uniquely representable as $\boldsymbol{x}=\lambda+\boldsymbol{c}$, where $\lambda \in \Lambda$ and $\boldsymbol{c} \in \mathbb{R}(\Lambda)$, i.e., there is a coset decomposition $\boldsymbol{R}^{N}= \Lambda+\mathbb{R}(\Lambda)$. Geometrically, this is a tesselation of $N$-space by translates of fundamental regions of $\Lambda$. While there is no unique fundamental region, every fundamental region $\mathbb{R}(\Lambda)$ must have the same volume $V(\Lambda)$ (if it is measurable), since it is congruent to any other fundamental region modulo $\Lambda$; this uniquely defines the fundamental volume $V(\Lambda)$. For example, one fundamental region of the onedimensional integer lattice $\boldsymbol{Z}$ is the half-open interval $[0,1)=\{c: 0 \leq c<1\}$; another is the half-open interval ( $-1 / 2,1 / 2$ ]. Whatever fundamental region we take, however, its volume (length) must be one. Similarly, for $\boldsymbol{Z}^{N}$, we may take $\mathbb{R}\left(\boldsymbol{Z}^{N}\right)$ as the half-open $N$-cube $[0,1)^{N}$ or as $(-1 / 2,1 / 2]^{N}$; again the volume $V\left(\boldsymbol{Z}^{N}\right)$ is one for any $N$. Happily, the computation of fundamental volumes of an integer lattice $\Lambda$ may be completely avoided by use of the following lemma, if we know the order $\left|\boldsymbol{Z}^{N} / \Lambda\right|$ of the partition $Z^{N} / \Lambda$. Lemma 1: If $\Lambda^{\prime}$ is a sublattice of $\Lambda$ of order $\left|\Lambda / \Lambda^{\prime}\right|$, then $V\left(\Lambda^{\prime}\right)=\left|\Lambda / \Lambda^{\prime}\right| V(\Lambda)$. Proof: Since only one of every $\left|\Lambda / \Lambda^{\prime}\right|$ points in $\Lambda$ is in $\Lambda^{\prime}$, the fundamental volume of $\Lambda^{\prime}$ must be $\left|\Lambda / \Lambda^{\prime}\right|$ times larger than that of $\Lambda$ for its fundamental regions to fill $N$-space. In fact, we may take a union of $\left|\Lambda / \Lambda^{\prime}\right|$ fundamental regions of $\Lambda$ as a fundamental region of $\Lambda^{\prime}$, one $(\mathbb{R}(\Lambda)+c)$ associated with each member $c$ of a set of coset representatives for $\Lambda / \Lambda^{\prime}$, in view of the decomposition chain $\boldsymbol{R}^{N}=\Lambda^{\prime}+\left[\Lambda / \Lambda^{\prime}\right]+\mathbb{R}(\Lambda)$. Corollary: If $\Lambda$ is an integer lattice, then $V(\Lambda)=\left|Z^{N} / \Lambda\right|$. Notice that Lemma 1 does not essentially depend on $\Lambda$ being a lattice; as long as $\Lambda$ is a union of some number $\left|\Lambda / \Lambda^{\prime}\right|$ of cosets of $\Lambda^{\prime}$, the points of $\Lambda$ are $\left|\Lambda / \Lambda^{\prime}\right|$ times as dense in $N$-space as those of $\Lambda^{\prime}$. From the two geometrical parameters $d_{\min }^{2}(\Lambda)$ and $V(\Lambda)$, we define the fundamental coding gain $\gamma(\Lambda)$ of a lattice $\Lambda$ as follows: $$ \gamma(\Lambda) \triangleq d_{\min }^{2}(\Lambda) / V(\Lambda)^{2 / N} $$ (in the mathematical literature this is called Hermite's parameter and is also denoted by the symbol $\gamma$ ). The fundamental coding gain is a normalized measure of the density of a lattice in the following various senses. a) It is dimensionless. Both $d_{\text {min }}^{2}(\Lambda)$ and $V(\Lambda)^{2 / N}$ have the dimensions of a two-dimensional volume (area). We shall often find that the most appropriate normalization of other parameters is to two dimensions. b) The fundamental coding gain is invariant to scaling, $\gamma(r \Lambda)=\gamma(\Lambda)$, because $d_{\text {min }}^{2}(r \Lambda)=r^{2} d_{\text {min }}^{2}(\Lambda)$ and $V(r \Lambda) =r^{N} V(\Lambda)$. c) More generally, the fundamental coding gain is invariant to any scaled orthogonal transformation $T, \gamma(T \Lambda) =\gamma(\Lambda)$, because $d_{\text {min }}^{2}(T \Lambda)=|\operatorname{det} T|^{2 / N} d_{\text {min }}^{2}(\Lambda)$ and $V(T \Lambda) =|\operatorname{det} T| V(\Lambda)$, where $\operatorname{det} T$ is the determinant of $T$. Thus any version of $\Lambda$ has the same fundamental coding gain. d) The fundamental coding gain is invariant to the Cartesian product operation, $\gamma\left(\Lambda^{M}\right)=\gamma(\Lambda)$, because $d_{\min }^{2}\left(\Lambda^{M}\right)=d_{\min }^{2}(\Lambda)$ and $V\left(\Lambda^{M}\right)=V(\Lambda)^{M}$. Thus if $\Lambda_{N}$ is an $N$-dimensional lattice and $\Lambda_{M}$ is an $M$-dimensional lattice, then in $M N$-space (where they can be compared directly) $\Lambda_{N}^{M}$ has a greater or lesser density of points per unit volume than does $\Lambda_{N}^{M}$ according to whether $\gamma\left(\Lambda_{N}\right)$ is greater or less than $\gamma\left(\Lambda_{M}\right)$, provided that they are scaled so that their minimum squared distances are the same. We therefore say that $\Lambda$ is denser than $\Lambda^{\prime}$ if $\gamma(\Lambda)>\gamma\left(\Lambda^{\prime}\right)$, regardless of whether $\Lambda$ and $\Lambda^{\prime}$ have the same dimension. e) For any $N, \gamma\left(\boldsymbol{Z}^{N}\right)=1$. An uncoded system may be defined as one that uses constellations based on $\boldsymbol{Z}^{N}$ (e.g., PAM uses constellations based on $\boldsymbol{Z}$, and narrow-sense quadrature amplitude modulation (QAM) uses constellations based on $\boldsymbol{Z}^{2}$ ). Thus the fundamental coding gain $\gamma(\Lambda)$ of an arbitrary lattice $\Lambda$ may be considered to be the gain using constellations based on $\Lambda$ over an uncoded system using constellations based on $\boldsymbol{Z}^{N}$. f) More concretely, suppose we form a $2^{n}$-point $N$ dimensional constellation by taking all points in an $N$ dimensional lattice $\Lambda$ (or a coset of $\Lambda$ ) that lie within an $N$-sphere with radius chosen just large enough to enclose the desired number of points. The volume of the sphere must then be about $2^{n} V(\Lambda)$ for large $n$. For the integer lattice $\boldsymbol{Z}^{N}$, the volume of such a sphere must be about $2^{n}$. The ratio of the radii of the two spheres is thus about $V(\Lambda)^{-1 / N}$ (this dimensional argument in fact holds for constellation boundaries of any shape). If we scale $\Lambda$ so that $d_{\text {min }}^{2}(\Lambda)=1$, then the minimum distance is the same as for $\boldsymbol{Z}^{N}$, but we achieve an average power reduction of about $V(\Lambda)^{-2 / N}$, or $\gamma(\Lambda)$, by using the constellation based on $\Lambda$ rather than that based on $\boldsymbol{Z}^{N}$. Thus $\gamma(\Lambda)$ is normalized properly to measure a power gain, and we will often give its value in decibels. For example, $R \boldsymbol{Z}^{2}$ is a version of $\boldsymbol{Z}^{2}$ with $d_{\text {min }}^{2}=2$ (the rotation operator $R$ always doubles norms, in any number of dimensions). The partition $\boldsymbol{Z}^{2} / R \boldsymbol{Z}^{2}$ has order 2, and thus $V\left(R Z^{2}\right)=2$. Thus we verify that $\gamma\left(R Z^{2}\right)=1$. As an example of a denser lattice, the Schläfli lattice $D_{4}$ may be defined as the four-dimensional integer lattice consisting of all integer 4 -tuples with an even number of odd coordinates or, equivalently, with even norm. The order of the partition $Z^{4} / D_{4}$ is two, because $Z^{4}$ is the union of $D_{4}$ and its coset $D_{4}+(1,0,0,0)$ (the set of all integer 4-tuples with an odd number of odd coordinates or, equivalently, with odd norm). Thus $V\left(D_{4}\right)=2$. Clearly, $d_{\text {min }}^{2}\left(D_{4}\right)=2$; therefore, the fundamental coding gain of $D_{4}$ is $$ \gamma\left(D_{4}\right)=2 / 2^{2 / 4}=2^{1 / 2} . $$ Thus $D_{4}$ is denser than $\boldsymbol{Z}$ or $\boldsymbol{Z}^{4}$ by a factor of $2^{1 / 2}$ (or 1.51 dB ). The elements of norm 2 are the 24 points obtained by permutations and coordinate sign changes of the 4-tuple ( $1,1,0,0$ ), so the error coefficient $N_{0}\left(D_{4}\right)$ is 24 . Another way of comparing the density of $D_{4}$ to that of $\boldsymbol{Z}^{4}$ is the following. The lattice $R \boldsymbol{Z}^{4}$ is a version of $\boldsymbol{Z}^{4}$ with $d_{\min }^{2}\left(R Z^{4}\right)=2$ (since $R$ doubles norms) and with $V\left(R Z^{4}\right)=4$ (since $\gamma\left(R Z^{4}\right)=1$ ). Moreover, $R Z^{4}$ is a sublattice of $D_{4}$, which must be of order 2 (by Lemma 1); in fact, $D_{4}$ is the union of $R \boldsymbol{Z}^{4}$ (the lattice of integer 4-tuples with even norms in both pairs of coordinates) and its coset $R Z^{4}+(1,0,1,0)$ (the set of integer 4-tuples with odd norms in both pairs of coordinates). However, $D_{4}$ has the same minimum squared distance as $R \boldsymbol{Z}^{4}$. Thus it is possible to take a version of $\boldsymbol{Z}^{4}$ and insert a translate of that version into the interstices between points without reducing the minimum distance. So $D_{4}$ has twice as many points as $R Z^{4}$ per unit volume, with no decrease in $d_{\text {min }}^{2}$; hence $D_{4}$ is twice as dense as $R Z^{4}$ in four dimensions, or $2^{1 / 2}$ times as dense per two dimensions, which is the normalization used in the definition of $\gamma$. We see that $Z^{4} / D_{4} / R Z^{4}$ is a chain of two-way partitions with distances $1 / 2 / 2$ (for short). However, since $D_{4}$ is a sublattice of $Z^{4}$, it follows that $R D_{4}$ is a sublattice of $R Z^{4}, R^{2} D_{4}=2 D_{4}$ is a sublattice of $R^{2} Z^{4}=2 Z^{4}$, and so forth. Hence $\boldsymbol{Z}^{4} / D_{4} / R \boldsymbol{Z}^{4} / R D_{4} / 2 \boldsymbol{Z}^{4} / 2 D_{4} / 2 R \boldsymbol{Z}^{4} / 2 R D_{4} / \cdots$ is an infinite chain of two-way partitions, with distances $1 / 2 / 2 / 4 / 4 / 8 / 8 / 16 / \cdots$. ## D. Complex Lattices and Gaussian Integers A complex lattice $\Lambda$ is a discrete set of points in complex Euclidean $N$-space $\boldsymbol{C}^{N}$ that forms a group under ordinary (complex) vector addition. Again, we stipulate that the only such lattices to be considered here will actually span $N$ dimensions, so we shall feel free to call such a $\Lambda$ an $N$-dimensional complex lattice. An obvious isomorphism (written $\Lambda_{r} \simeq \Lambda_{c}$ ) exists between any $2 N$-dimensional real lattice $\Lambda_{r}$ and a corresponding $N$-dimensional complex lattice $\Lambda_{c}$, formed by taking each pair of coordinates of $\Lambda_{r}$ to specify the real and imaginary parts of each coordinate of $\Lambda_{c}$, or vice versa. Addition of two points gives the same result in either case. Sublattices, cosets, and all such group properties carry over. Even the norm of two corresponding vectors is the same, so distances are not affected. Thus for most purposes it makes no difference whether we consider a lattice to be real or complex. For all parameters previously defined (e.g., $d_{\text {min }}^{2}(\Lambda), V(\Lambda), \gamma(\Lambda)$ ), we may define the values for a complex lattice to be the same as those for the corresponding real lattice. The only difference of any significance arises when we consider multiplicative operations, such as scaling, or the taking of inner products. A complex lattice $\Lambda_{c}$ may be scaled by either a real number $r$ or a complex number $\alpha$, the latter operation involving an equal phase rotation of each coordinate of $\Lambda_{c}$ by the phase of $\alpha$ (as well as a scaling of lengths by $|\alpha|$, or norms by $|\alpha|^{2}$ ). The inner product $(x, y)$ of two real vectors $x$ and $y$ is the sum of the products of their coordinates and must be real; the (Hermitian) inner product ( $\boldsymbol{x}, \boldsymbol{y}$ ) of two complex vectors $\boldsymbol{x}$ and $y$ is the sum of the products of the coordinates of $x$ with the complex conjugates of the coordinates of $y$ and may be complex. Thus there may arise differences in definitions of orthogonality, duality, and so forth. In general, for the lattices considered in this paper, we shall prefer the complex definitions. The simplest example of a complex lattice is the onedimensional complex lattice $\boldsymbol{G}$ corresponding to the twodimensional real lattice $\boldsymbol{Z}^{2}$. The point ( $a, b$ ) in $\boldsymbol{Z}^{2}$ corre- sponds to the point $a+b i$ in $\boldsymbol{G}$, where $a$ and $b$ may be any pair of integers. The set $\boldsymbol{G}$ is called the set of Gaussian integers. The Gaussian integers $\boldsymbol{G}$ actually form a system of complex integers analogous to the ordinary real integers $\boldsymbol{Z}$. Multiplication of two elements of $\boldsymbol{G}$ (using complex arithmetic) yields another element of $\boldsymbol{G}$, which cannot be 0 unless one of the two elements is 0 (in fact, their norms multiply as real integers). Thus $\boldsymbol{G}$ is a ring and, in fact, an integral domain. Indeed, we have unique factorization in $\boldsymbol{G}$ : every element of $\boldsymbol{G}$ can be expressed uniquely as a product of primes, up to units, where the units (invertible elements) are $\pm 1$ and $\pm i$, and the primes are the elements that have no divisors other than themselves, up to units. The primes of $\boldsymbol{G}$, in order of increasing norm, are $1+i$, $2 \pm i, 3, \cdots$, with norms $2,5,9, \cdots$. We denote the prime of least norm by $\phi \triangleq 1+i$. (Note that $|\phi|^{2}=\phi \phi^{*}=2$, and thus two is not a prime in $\boldsymbol{G}$.) We may scale $\boldsymbol{G}$ by any element $g \in \boldsymbol{G}$ and obtain a sublattice $g \boldsymbol{G}$ of $\boldsymbol{G}$. By Lemma 1, the partition $\boldsymbol{G} / g \boldsymbol{G}$ must have order $|g|^{2}$ (the norm of $g$ ). There are thus $|g|^{2}$ equivalence classes of $\boldsymbol{G}$ modulo $g$. For example, $\phi \boldsymbol{G}$ is a sublattice of $\boldsymbol{G}$ of order $|\phi|^{2}=2$ and, in fact, is the complex lattice corresponding to the real lattice $R \boldsymbol{Z}^{2}$. As with $R \boldsymbol{Z}^{2}, \phi \boldsymbol{G}$ consists of all the elements of $\boldsymbol{G}$ with even norm, its coset $\phi \boldsymbol{G}+1$ consists of all the elements of $\boldsymbol{G}$ with odd norm, and the union of $\phi \boldsymbol{G}$ and $\boldsymbol{\phi} \boldsymbol{G}+1$ is $\boldsymbol{G}$ (Fig. 4 may equally well be taken to illustrate this partition of $\boldsymbol{G}$ ). The coset representatives $[\boldsymbol{G} / \phi \boldsymbol{G}]$ may thus be taken as $\{0,1\}$, and are isomorphic to $\boldsymbol{Z}_{2}=\mathrm{GF}(2)$ using modulo $\phi$ arithmetic (since $2 \equiv 0 \bmod \phi$ ). More generally, $\phi^{\mu} \boldsymbol{G}$ is a sublattice of $\boldsymbol{G}$ of order $|\phi|^{2 \mu}=2^{\mu}$ and, in fact, is the complex lattice corresponding to the real lattice $R^{\mu} \boldsymbol{Z}^{2}$, which is equal to $2^{\mu / 2} \boldsymbol{Z}^{2}$ for $\mu$ even and $2^{(\mu-1) / 2} R Z^{2}$ for $\mu$ odd. As with $R^{\mu} Z^{2}, \phi^{\mu} G$ consists of all the elements of $\boldsymbol{G}$ whose norms are multiples of $2^{\mu}$, and thus $d_{\text {min }}^{2}\left(\phi^{\mu} G\right)=2^{\mu}$. There is then an infinite chain $\boldsymbol{G} / \phi \boldsymbol{G} / \phi^{2} \boldsymbol{G} / \phi^{3} \boldsymbol{G} / \phi^{4} \boldsymbol{G} / \cdots$ of two-way partitions with distances $1 / 2 / 4 / 8 / 16 / \cdots$, corresponding to the real chain $\boldsymbol{Z}^{2} / R \boldsymbol{Z}^{2} / 2 \boldsymbol{Z}^{2} / 2 R \boldsymbol{Z}^{2} / 4 \boldsymbol{Z}^{2} / \cdots$. In analogy to the chain $\boldsymbol{Z} / 2 \boldsymbol{Z} / 4 \boldsymbol{Z} / \cdots$, this chain suggests a complex binary representation of a Gaussian integer $g$ : $$ g=a_{0}+\phi a_{1}+\phi^{2} a_{2}+\cdots $$ where $a_{0}, a_{1}, a_{2}, \cdots \in\{0,1\}$, and $a_{0}$ specifies the coset of $\phi \boldsymbol{G}$ in the partition $\boldsymbol{G} / \phi \boldsymbol{G}, \phi a_{1}$ specifies the coset of $\phi^{2} \boldsymbol{G}$ in the partition $\phi \boldsymbol{G} / \phi^{2} \boldsymbol{G}$, and so forth. That is, the complex binary representation is based on the coset decomposition $$ \boldsymbol{G}=[\boldsymbol{G} / \phi \boldsymbol{G}]+\left[\phi \boldsymbol{G} / \phi^{2} \boldsymbol{G}\right]+\left[\phi^{2} \boldsymbol{G} / \phi^{3} \boldsymbol{G}\right]+\cdots $$ For any lattice $\Lambda$, if $\lambda$ is any lattice point and $m$ is any integer, then $\pm m \lambda= \pm(\lambda+\lambda+\cdots+\lambda)$ is a lattice point, so $m \Lambda$ is a sublattice of $\Lambda$, and $\Lambda$ (like any additive group) is a module over the ring $\boldsymbol{Z}$ of ordinary integers. However, a complex lattice $\Lambda$ is not necessarily a module over the ring $\boldsymbol{G}$ of Gaussian integers (for example, the two-dimensional hexagonal lattice is not). It is so if and only if $\lambda \in \Lambda$ implies $i \lambda \in \Lambda$; for then if $g=a+b i$ is any Gaussian integer, $g \lambda=a \lambda+b(i \lambda)$ is a lattice point. Then $g \Lambda$ is a sublattice of $\Lambda$ for any $g \in \boldsymbol{G}$. In particular, $i \Lambda$ is a sublattice of $\Lambda$; but since $i(i \Lambda)=-\Lambda=\Lambda$ is a sublattice of $i \Lambda$, in fact $i \Lambda=\Lambda$. When necessary, we shall call such a complex lattice a $\boldsymbol{G}$-lattice. In general, multiplication of a $\boldsymbol{G}$-lattice $\Lambda_{\mathrm{c}}$ by the complex scalar $\phi$ has much the same effect as a transformation of the corresponding real lattice $\Lambda_{r}$ by the rotation operator $R$. The correspondence is not exact because $R$ includes a reflection as well as rotation and scaling, so that $R^{2}=2$, whereas $\phi^{2}=2 i$. (We could have avoided this difficulty by exchanging columns in the definition of $R$.) However, if $\Lambda=\Lambda^{*}$-i.e., if $\lambda \in \Lambda$ implies $\lambda^{*} \in \Lambda$, where $\lambda^{*}$ is the complex conjugate of $\lambda$-as will be true for all lattices to be considered here, then $\phi \Lambda_{\mathrm{c}} \simeq R \Lambda_{r}$. The difference is slight, but we regard multiplication by the complex scalar $\phi$ as fundamentally a more natural operation than rotation by $R$. ## E. Binary Lattices Binary lattices have proved to be the most useful class of lattices in applications. On the one hand, this is because they are a natural extension of binary block codes and are well suited to the bit-oriented real world. On the other hand, in many cases they give the best performance, both as lattices and as the basis of trellis codes, a result which would have been harder to predict a priori. For instance, the densest known lattices in $1,4,8,16$, and 24 dimensions (among others) are binary lattices. We provide a brief introduction here; part II discusses binary lattices in more detail. A real $N$-dimensional lattice $\Lambda$ is a binary lattice if it is an integer lattice that has $2^{m} Z^{N}$ as a sublattice for some $m$. The least such $m$ is called the 2-depth of the lattice. Thus $\boldsymbol{Z}^{N} / \Lambda / 2^{m} \boldsymbol{Z}^{N}$ is a partition chain. It turns out that all of the binary lattices that have proved to be useful to date have 2 -depth equal to one or two; we shall call such lattices mod-2 and mod-4 lattices, respectively. A complex $N$-dimensional lattice $\Lambda$ is a binary lattice if it is a Gaussian integer $\boldsymbol{G}$-lattice that has $\phi^{\mu} \boldsymbol{G}^{N}$ as a sublattice for some $\mu$. The least such $\mu$ is called the $\phi$-depth of the lattice. Thus $G^{N} / \Lambda / \phi^{\mu} G^{N}$ is a partition chain. If $\Lambda$ is a $2 N$-dimensional real binary lattice, then the corresponding $N$-dimensional complex lattice is also a complex binary lattice (if it is a $\boldsymbol{G}$-lattice), and vice versa, since $2^{m} \boldsymbol{Z}^{2 N} \simeq \phi^{2 m} \boldsymbol{G}^{N} \subset \phi^{2 m-1} \boldsymbol{G}^{N}$. So we may speak of the $\phi$-depth of a real $2 N$-dimensional binary lattice. A real $2 N$-dimensional binary lattice with 2 -depth $m$ has $\phi$-depth $2 m$ or $2 m-1$; thus the $\phi$-depth is twice as fine-grained a parameter, and we shall henceforth call it simply the depth $\mu$ of a binary lattice. A mod-2 binary lattice thus has depth 1 or 2 , and a mod-4 binary lattice has depth 3 or 4 . For example, since $\boldsymbol{Z}^{4} / D_{4} / R \boldsymbol{Z}^{4}=\boldsymbol{G}^{2} / D_{4} / \phi \boldsymbol{G}^{2}$ is a partition chain, $D_{4}$ is a mod-2 binary lattice with depth $\mu=1$. Since the order of the partition $Z^{N} / 2^{m} Z^{N}$ (resp. $\boldsymbol{G}^{N} / \phi^{\mu} \boldsymbol{G}^{N}$ ) is a power of two, the orders of $\boldsymbol{Z}^{N} / \Lambda$ and $\Lambda / 2^{m} \boldsymbol{Z}^{N}$ (resp. $\boldsymbol{G}^{N} / \Lambda$ and $\Lambda / \phi^{\mu} \boldsymbol{G}^{N}$ ) must be powers of two, since their product is $\left|\boldsymbol{Z}^{N} / 2^{m} \boldsymbol{Z}^{N}\right|$ (resp. $\left|\boldsymbol{G}^{N} / \phi^{\mu} \boldsymbol{G}^{N}\right|$ ). The redundancy $r(\Lambda)$ of a binary lattice $\Lambda$ is defined as the binary logarithm of $\left|Z^{N} / \Lambda\right|$, so that $\left|Z^{N} / \Lambda\right|=2^{r(\Lambda)}$. In view of the corollary to Lemma 1, the fundamental volume of a binary lattice is therefore $V(\Lambda)=2^{r(\Lambda)}$, and the fundamental coding gain is $$ \gamma(\Lambda)=2^{-\rho(\Lambda)} d_{\min }^{2}(\Lambda) $$ where $\rho(\Lambda)$ is the normalized redundancy (per two dimensions) of $\Lambda, \rho(\Lambda)=r(\Lambda) / N$, where $2 N$ is the dimension of $\Lambda$ as a real lattice, or $N$ is the dimension of $\Lambda$ as a complex lattice. If we choose a constellation of (say) $2^{n}$ points from $\Lambda$, they will occupy a volume in $2 N$-space approximately $2^{r(\Lambda)}$ times as large as the same number of points chosen from $Z^{2 N}$ would. We therefore say that the constellation expansion factor is $2^{r(\Lambda)}$ in $2 N$ dimensions, or $2^{\rho(\Lambda)}$ in two dimensions. As previously discussed, this translates into a power cost due to constellation expansion of a factor of $2^{\rho(\Lambda)}$, or $\rho(\Lambda) \cdot 3.01 \mathrm{~dB}$. The formula for fundamental coding gain just given therefore has an interpretation as follows: the minimum squared distance gain of a factor of $d_{\text {min }}^{2}(\Lambda)$ (relative to $d_{\text {min }}^{2}\left(\boldsymbol{Z}^{N}\right)=1$ ) is partially offset by a constellation expansion power cost of a factor of $2^{-\rho(\Lambda)}$, leaving a net coding gain of $\gamma(\Lambda)=2^{-\rho(\Lambda)} d_{\text {min }}^{2}(\Lambda)$. The order of $\Lambda / 2^{m} \boldsymbol{Z}^{2 N}$ is $2^{2 N m-r(\Lambda)}$, and the order of $\Lambda / \phi^{\mu} \boldsymbol{G}^{N}$ is $2^{N \mu-r(\Lambda)}$. We may give $k(\Lambda) \triangleq N \mu(\Lambda)-r(\Lambda)$ the somewhat ugly but dual name of the 'informativity' of $\Lambda$, where $N$ is the dimension of $\Lambda$ as a complex lattice; the normalized informativity (per two dimensions) of $\Lambda$ is $\kappa(\Lambda) \triangleq k(\Lambda) / N$, where $N$ is the dimension of $\Lambda$ as a complex lattice, or $2 N$ is the dimension of $\Lambda$ as a real lattice. Thus the depth of a binary lattice $\Lambda$ is the sum of its normalized redundancy and informativity: $$ \mu(\Lambda)=\rho(\Lambda)+\kappa(\Lambda) . $$ For example, the depth of $D_{4}$ is 1 , its redundancy and informativity are both equal to 1 , and its normalized redundancy and informativity are both equal to $1 / 2$. Moreover, if a sublattice $\Lambda^{\prime}$ of a binary lattice $\Lambda$ is also a binary lattice, then $\Lambda / \Lambda^{\prime}$ is a partition whose order is a power of two, since $\boldsymbol{Z}^{N} / \Lambda / \Lambda^{\prime} / 2^{m} \boldsymbol{Z}^{N}$ is a partition chain for some $m$. We define the depth of a partition $\Lambda / \Lambda^{\prime}$ of binary lattices as the depth of $\Lambda^{\prime}, \mu\left(\Lambda / \Lambda^{\prime}\right) \triangleq \mu\left(\Lambda^{\prime}\right)$; the redundancy as the redundancy of $\Lambda, \rho\left(\Lambda / \Lambda^{\prime}\right) \triangleq \rho(\Lambda)$; and the informativity as the informativity of $\Lambda^{\prime}, \kappa\left(\Lambda / \Lambda^{\prime}\right) \triangleq \kappa\left(\Lambda^{\prime}\right)$. It follows that the order of the partition is $$ \left|\Lambda / \Lambda^{\prime}\right|=2^{N\left(\mu\left(\Lambda^{\prime}\right)-\rho(\Lambda)-\kappa\left(\Lambda^{\prime}\right)\right)} $$ where $2 N$ is the dimension of $\Lambda$ as a real lattice. ## F. Labelings of Partitions of Binary Lattices If $\Lambda$ and $\Lambda^{\prime}$ are binary lattices such that $\Lambda^{\prime}$ is a sublattice of $\Lambda$, then the partition $\Lambda / \Lambda^{\prime}$ has order $2^{K}$ for some integer $K$. Any map from binary $K$-tuples $\boldsymbol{a}$ to unique cosets of $\Lambda^{\prime}$ in this partition is called a labeling. The labeling may be defined by a function $\boldsymbol{c}(\boldsymbol{a})$, where $\boldsymbol{a}$ is any binary $K$-tuple, called a label, and $\boldsymbol{c}(\boldsymbol{a})$ is a coset representative of the coset of $\Lambda^{\prime}$ specified by $\boldsymbol{a}$. We always assume that $\boldsymbol{c ( 0 )}=\mathbf{0}$, i.e., that the zero label maps to the zero coset, namely, $\Lambda^{\prime}$ itself. The following lemma shows, first, that any such partition can be broken up into a chain of $K$ two-way partitions $\Lambda_{k} / \Lambda_{k+1}, 0 \leq k \leq K-1$; second, that there is then a labeling $$ \boldsymbol{c}(\boldsymbol{a})=\sum a_{k} \boldsymbol{g}_{k} $$ where $a_{k}$ is the $k$ th coordinate of the binary $K$-tuple $a$ and $\boldsymbol{g}_{k}$ is an element of $\Lambda_{k}$ but not of $\Lambda_{k+1}$ such that the two vectors $\left\{a_{k} \boldsymbol{g}_{k}, a_{k} \in\{0,1\}\right\}$, are a system of coset representatives for the cosets of $\Lambda_{k+1}$ in the two-way partition $\Lambda_{k} / \Lambda_{k+1}$. Thus the $2^{K}$ binary linear combinations $\left\{\sum a_{k} \boldsymbol{g}_{k}\right\}$ of the generators $\boldsymbol{g}_{k}$ are a system of coset representatives $\left[\Lambda_{k} / \Lambda_{k+1}\right]$ for the partition $\Lambda_{k} / \Lambda_{k+1}$ (this is a special case of a general result for groups of order $2^{K}-$ binary groups-discussed in part II). In the coding context, we call such a labeling an Ungerboeck labeling. Lemma 2: Let $\Lambda$ and $\Lambda^{\prime}$ be binary lattices such that $\Lambda^{\prime}$ is a sublattice of $\Lambda$, and let $\left|\Lambda / \Lambda^{\prime}\right|=2^{K}$. Then there is a sequence of lattices $\Lambda_{0}=\Lambda, \Lambda_{1}, \cdots, \Lambda_{K}=\Lambda^{\prime}$ such that $\Lambda_{0} / \Lambda_{1} / \cdots / \Lambda_{K}$ is a lattice partition chain and each partition $\Lambda_{k} / \Lambda_{k+1}$ is two-way, $0 \leq k \leq K-1 . \Lambda$ has the coset decomposition $$ \Lambda=\Lambda^{\prime}+\left\{\sum a_{k} \boldsymbol{g}_{k}\right\} $$ where $a_{k} \in\{0,1\}$ and $\left\{a_{k} g_{k}, a_{k} \in\{0,1\}\right\}$ is a system of coset representatives for $\left[\Lambda_{k} / \Lambda_{k+1}\right], 0 \leq k \leq K-1$. Sketch of proof (by induction, from $k=K-1$ down to $k=0$ ): Assume that $\Lambda_{k+1}$ is a binary lattice such that $\Lambda / \Lambda_{k+1} / \Lambda^{\prime}$ is a partition chain; $\Lambda_{K}=\Lambda^{\prime}$ is certainly such a lattice. If $\Lambda_{k+1} \neq \Lambda$, then a vector $\boldsymbol{g}_{k}$ exists in $\Lambda$ that is not in $\Lambda_{k+1}$ but has order $2 \bmod \Lambda_{k+1}$, i.e., such that $\boldsymbol{g}_{k}+\boldsymbol{g}_{k} \in \Lambda_{k+1}$ (see part II). Let $\Lambda_{k}$ then be the union of $\Lambda_{k+1}$ and its coset $\Lambda_{k+1}+\boldsymbol{g}_{k} ; \Lambda_{k}$ is clearly a lattice, which by construction is a sublattice of $\Lambda$ and has $\Lambda_{k+1}$ (and thus $\Lambda^{\prime}$ ) as a sublattice. The two vectors $\left\{a_{k} g_{k}, a_{k} \in\right. \{0,1\}\}$ are a system of coset representatives for the cosets of $\Lambda_{k+1}$ in the two-way partition $\Lambda_{k} / \Lambda_{k+1}$. By induction, the $2^{K-k}$ binary linear combinations $\left\{\Sigma_{k \leq j \leq K-1} a_{j} \boldsymbol{g}_{j}\right\}$ are a system of coset representatives for $\Lambda_{k} / \Lambda^{\prime}$. The induction terminates when $k=0$. Fig. 6 portrays an Ungerboeck labeling in two ways: as a partition tower, as in Fig. 5, and as a binary partition tree. In the tower, the first bit $a_{0}$ in the label $\boldsymbol{a}$ selects one ![](/documents/a12a64b5-5d83-44f3-b724-a58cf3053e87/images/image_006.jpg) Fig. 6. Illustration of Ungerboeck labeling by partition tower and by partition tree. of the two cosets of $\Lambda_{1}$ in the partition $\Lambda / \Lambda_{1}$, the second bit $a_{1}$ selects one of the two cosets of $\Lambda_{2}$ in the partition $\Lambda_{1} / \Lambda_{2}$, and so forth. In the tree, these are shown as two-way branches. Thus an Ungerboeck labeling is nested, in the sense that the first $k$ bits of the labeling of the partition $\Lambda / \Lambda^{\prime}$ are a labeling of the partition $\Lambda / \Lambda_{k}$. If $\lambda$ is a vector in $\Lambda$ in the coset of $\Lambda^{\prime}$ whose label is $\boldsymbol{a}$, then $\lambda$ is in a coset of $\Lambda_{k}$ determined by the first $k$ bits of $\boldsymbol{a}$. Consequently, we have the lattice/coset version of the Ungerboeck distance bound: if $\lambda$ and $\lambda^{\prime}$ are two different points of $\Lambda$ that lie in cosets of $\Lambda^{\prime}$ whose labels agree in their first $k$ bits, then $\left\|\lambda-\lambda^{\prime}\right\|^{2} \geq d_{\min }^{2}\left(\Lambda_{k}\right)$, because both $\lambda$ and $\lambda^{\prime}$ are in the same coset of $\Lambda_{k}$ (this is a special case of the partition distance lemma of part II). ## G. Decomposition of Binary Lattices This section shows that the structure of binary lattices (whether real or complex) is a generalization of that of binary block codes. Mod-2 binary lattices are essentially isomorphic to linear binary block codes, as we can see in the following lemma and proof (this is "Construction A" of Leech and Sloane [6]). Recall that an ( $N, K$ ) linear binary block code is any $K$-dimensional subspace of the $N$-dimensional linear vector space over $\boldsymbol{Z}_{2}=\mathrm{GF}(2)$ of all binary $N$-tuples, where the coordinates of each of the $2^{K}$ codewords are regarded as elements of the binary field $\boldsymbol{Z}_{2}=\mathrm{GF}(2)$. The codewords may be expressed as linear combinations $\boldsymbol{c}(\boldsymbol{a})=\sum a_{k} \boldsymbol{g}_{k}$ of a set of $K$ binary $N$-tuples $\boldsymbol{g}_{k}, 1 \leq k \leq K$, called generators. The set is called the generator matrix $G$, so $\boldsymbol{c}(\boldsymbol{a})=\boldsymbol{a} G$. The minimum Hamming distance of the code is the minimum number of nonzero coordinates in any nonzero codeword; we shall sometimes call an ( $N, K$ ) code with minimum Hamming distance $d_{H}$ an ( $N, K, d_{H}$ ) code. (Unless otherwise specified, an $(N, K)$ code will always mean a linear binary ( $N, K$ ) code.) Lemma 3: An $N$-dimensional real lattice $\Lambda$ is a mod-2 binary lattice if and only if it is the set of all integer $N$-tuples that are congruent modulo 2 to one of the codewords $\boldsymbol{c}$ in a linear binary ( $N, K$ ) block code $C$. The redundancy of $\Lambda$ is $r(\Lambda)=N-K$, and its minimum squared distance is $d_{\min }^{2}(\Lambda)=\min \left[4, d_{H}(C)\right]$, where $d_{H}(C)$ is the minimum Hamming distance of the code $C$. Proof: If $\Lambda$ is a mod- 2 binary lattice, then $\boldsymbol{Z}^{N} / \Lambda / 2 \boldsymbol{Z}^{N}$ is a partition chain, $\Lambda / 2 \boldsymbol{Z}^{N}$ has order $2^{K}$ for some integer $K$, and $\boldsymbol{Z}^{N} / \Lambda$ then has order $2^{N-K}$; so the redundancy of $\Lambda$ is $r(\Lambda)=N-K . \Lambda$ is thus the union of $2^{K}$ cosets of $2 Z^{N}$ (in the partition $Z^{N} / 2 Z^{N}$ ); any such coset of $2 \boldsymbol{Z}^{N}$ has a binary coset representative $\boldsymbol{c}$, i.e., an $N$ tuple of ones and zeros, and the coset $2 \boldsymbol{Z}^{N}+\boldsymbol{c}$ consists of all integer $N$-tuples congruent to $c$ modulo 2 . By Lemma 2, $\Lambda=2 \boldsymbol{Z}^{N}+\left\{\sum a_{k} \boldsymbol{g}_{k}\right\}$, where the $\boldsymbol{g}_{k}$ can be taken as binary $N$-tuples, $1 \leq k \leq K$, and addition may be taken modulo $2 \boldsymbol{Z}^{N}$; i.e., modulo 2 . The set $C$ of $2^{K}$ coset representatives $\left\{\boldsymbol{c}(\boldsymbol{a})=\sum a_{k} \boldsymbol{g}_{k}\right\}$ is thus a linear binary ( $N, K$ ) block code. Conversely, if $C$ is such a code, then the set of all integer $N$-tuples that are congruent modulo 2 to code- words $\boldsymbol{c}$ in $C$ is easily seen to be a lattice, with $2 \boldsymbol{Z}^{N}$ as a sublattice (the set of all integer $N$-tuples congruent to $\mathbf{0}$ modulo 2). If $\boldsymbol{c} \neq \mathbf{0}$, the minimum norm in the coset $2 \boldsymbol{Z}^{N}+\boldsymbol{c}$ is the norm $\|\boldsymbol{c}\|^{2}$ of $\boldsymbol{c}$ itself, which is also the Hamming weight of $c$. The minimum such weight is the minimum Hamming distance of the code, $d_{H}(C)$. If $\boldsymbol{c}=\mathbf{0}$, the minimum nonzero norm in $2 Z^{N}$ itself is four. Thus $d_{\text {min }}^{2}(\Lambda)= \min \left[4, d_{H}(C)\right]$. Lemma 3 says that any mod-2 lattice $\Lambda$ may be constructed as a coset code $\mathbb{C}\left(\boldsymbol{Z}^{N} / 2 \boldsymbol{Z}^{N} ; C\right)$, where $C$ is a linear binary ( $N, K$ ) block code. We can express $\Lambda$ by the code formula (coset decomposition) $\Lambda=2 \boldsymbol{Z}^{N}+C$. This explicitly exhibits $\Lambda$ as the union of $2^{K}$ cosets of $2 \boldsymbol{Z}^{N}$. The labeling $\boldsymbol{c}(\boldsymbol{a})=\Sigma a_{k} \boldsymbol{g}_{k}=\boldsymbol{a} G(\bmod 2)$ is linear in the sense that $\boldsymbol{c}\left(\boldsymbol{a} \oplus \boldsymbol{a}^{\prime}\right)=\boldsymbol{c}(\boldsymbol{a}) \oplus \boldsymbol{c}\left(\boldsymbol{a}^{\prime}\right)$. This picture of a mod-2 lattice as a coset code is illustrated in Fig. 7. ![](/documents/a12a64b5-5d83-44f3-b724-a58cf3053e87/images/image_007.jpg) Fig. 7. Illustration of mod- 2 binary lattice $\Lambda$ as union of $2^{K}$ cosets of $2 \boldsymbol{Z}^{N}$, each coset corresponding to codeword $c(\boldsymbol{a})$ in linear binary $(N, K)$ code $C$. For example, the mod-2 lattices $\boldsymbol{Z}^{2}, R \boldsymbol{Z}^{2}$, and $2 \boldsymbol{Z}^{2}$ correspond to the $(2,2,1),(2,1,2)$, and $(2,0, \infty)$ binary codes, respectively, and consequently have minimum squared distances 1,2 , and 4 . The mod-2 lattices $Z^{4}, D_{4}$, $R \boldsymbol{Z}^{4}, R D_{4}$ and $2 \boldsymbol{Z}^{4}$ correspond to $(4,4,1),(4,3,2),(4,2,2) =(2,1,2)^{2},(4,1,4)$, and $(4,0, \infty)$ binary codes, with distances $1,2,2,4$, and 4. In general, the lattice corresponding to any single-par-ity-check ( $N, N-1,2$ ) code is a mod-2 lattice, the so-called "checkerboard lattice" $D_{N}$, with minimum squared distance 2 and redundancy 1 , a sublattice of $\boldsymbol{Z}^{N}$ of order 2. The lattice $D_{2}$ is the lattice $R \boldsymbol{Z}^{2} \simeq \phi \boldsymbol{G}$. Mod-2 lattices are limited to minimum squared distances of four or less because $N$-tuples in $2 \boldsymbol{Z}^{N}$ such as $(2,0, \cdots, 0)$ are lattice points. Binary block codes with Hamming distance 4 are therefore of special interest. The Gosset lattice $E_{8}$ is the mod-2 lattice corresponding to the $(8,4,4)$ Reed-Muller code; $E_{8}$ thus has $d_{\text {min }}^{2}\left(E_{8}\right)=4$, $r\left(E_{8}\right)=4, \rho\left(E_{8}\right)=1$, and thus $\gamma\left(E_{8}\right)=2^{-1} \cdot 4=2$ ( 3.01 dB ). ( $E_{8}$ has many remarkable properties; it is perhaps the second most important lattice in lattice theory.) If $\Lambda^{\prime}$ is a sublattice of $\Lambda$, where both are mod- 2 lattices, it is easy to see that the code $C^{\prime}$ associated with $\Lambda^{\prime}$ must be a subcode of the code $C$ associated with $\Lambda$. In general, if $\Lambda$ is a mod-4 lattice, then $4 \boldsymbol{Z}^{N}$ is a sublattice, and Lemma 2 allows us to write $$ \Lambda=4 \boldsymbol{Z}^{N}+\left\{\sum a_{k} \boldsymbol{g}_{k}\right\} $$ where each $\boldsymbol{g}_{k}$ may be taken as a coset representative of $4 Z^{N}$, i.e., as an $N$-tuple of integers modulo 4 , but where the label $\boldsymbol{a}$ is still a $\{0,1\}$-valued integer $K$-tuple. Addition may be taken modulo 4 . This exhibits $\Lambda$ as a union of $2^{K}$ cosets of $4 \boldsymbol{Z}^{N}$, where the coset representatives are $\boldsymbol{c}(\boldsymbol{a})= \sum a_{k} \boldsymbol{g}_{k}(\bmod 4)$. If we take the coordinates of $\boldsymbol{c}(\boldsymbol{a})$ to be from the set $\{0, \pm 1,2\}$, then $\boldsymbol{c}(\boldsymbol{a})$ is also a coset leader of its coset, i.e., an element of minimum norm. For a further refinement, let $\Lambda_{e}$ be the set of all points in $\Lambda$ whose coordinates are all even. Then $\Lambda_{e}$ is a lattice, a sublattice of $\Lambda$, with $\boldsymbol{4} \boldsymbol{Z}^{N}$ as a sublattice, so $\Lambda / \Lambda_{e} / \boldsymbol{4} \boldsymbol{Z}^{N}$ is a partition chain, and there is a coset decomposition of the form $\Lambda=\boldsymbol{4} \boldsymbol{Z}^{N}+\left[\Lambda_{e} / \boldsymbol{4} \boldsymbol{Z}^{N}\right]+\left[\Lambda / \Lambda_{e}\right]$. The lattice $\Lambda_{e}$ is clearly a mod-2 lattice scaled by a factor of 2 ; consequently, $\Lambda_{e}=4 \boldsymbol{Z}^{N}+2 C$, where $C$ is a binary ( $N, K^{\prime}$ ) code for some $K^{\prime}$, by Lemma 3; in other words, the coset representatives $\left[\Lambda_{e} / \boldsymbol{4} \boldsymbol{Z}^{N}\right]$ may be taken as $2 C$, or $\left\{2 \sum a_{k} \boldsymbol{g}_{k}\right\}$, where the $\boldsymbol{g}_{k}$ constitute a set of $K^{\prime}$ binary generators for the code $C$. Thus we may take $K^{\prime}$ of the generators to be $2 \boldsymbol{g}_{k}, 1 \leq k \leq K^{\prime}$, and we may write $$ \Lambda=4 \boldsymbol{Z}^{N}+2 C+\left\{\sum a_{k} \boldsymbol{g}_{k}\right\} $$ where the $\boldsymbol{g}_{k}, K^{\prime}+1 \leq k \leq K$, are $N$-tuples of integers modulo 4 that are not all even, such that $\left\{\sum a_{k} \boldsymbol{g}_{k}\right\}$ is a system of coset representatives $\left[\Lambda / \Lambda_{e}\right]$. The generators $\left\{2 \boldsymbol{g}_{k}, K^{\prime}+1 \leq k \leq K\right\}$ generate a lattice $\Lambda^{\prime}$ that is a sublattice of $\Lambda_{e}$, whose elements are congruent to $2 \boldsymbol{c}^{\prime}$ modulo 4, where $c^{\prime}$ is a codeword in a binary ( $N, K-K^{\prime}$ ) block code $C^{\prime}$ that is a subcode of the code $C$, so $K-K^{\prime} \leq K^{\prime}$. It is sometimes possible to find a set of generators $\left\{\boldsymbol{g}_{k}, K^{\prime}+1 \leq k \leq K\right\}$ for $\Lambda / \Lambda_{e}$ such that each $\boldsymbol{g}_{k}$ is an $N$-tuple of ones and zeros and such that the "carries" (the twos-coefficients in the vector sum) of any sum $\boldsymbol{g}_{k}+\boldsymbol{g}_{k^{\prime}}$ are a codeword in $C$; then we say that $\Lambda$ is decomposable. The coset decomposition then becomes the code formula $\Lambda=4 \boldsymbol{Z}^{N}+2 C_{1}+C_{0}$, where $C_{0}$ is a subcode of $C_{1}$. This means that $\Lambda$ consists of all integer $N$-tuples whose coordinate ones-coefficients in the standard binary representation form a binary $N$-tuple $a_{0}$ that is a codeword in $C_{0}$, and whose coordinate twos-coefficients form a binary $N$ tuple $\boldsymbol{a}_{1}$ that is a codeword in $C_{1}$ (this is the "coordinate array" idea of Leech and Sloane [6]). The minimum squared distance of a mod-4 decomposable real lattice $\Lambda$ is $$ d_{\min }^{2}(\Lambda)=\min \left[16,4 d_{H}\left(C_{1}\right), d_{H}\left(C_{0}\right)\right] $$ because, on the one hand, there are $N$-tuples in $\boldsymbol{4 Z}^{N}$ of norm 16, in $2 C_{1}$ of norm $4 d_{H}\left(C_{1}\right)$ and in $C_{0}$ of norm $d_{H}\left(C_{0}\right)$; on the other hand, if $\lambda \equiv 2 a_{1}+a_{0} \bmod 4$ and $\boldsymbol{a}_{0} \neq \mathbf{0}$, then $\|\lambda\|^{2} \geq d_{H}\left(C_{0}\right)$; if $\boldsymbol{a}_{0}=\mathbf{0}$ but $\boldsymbol{a}_{1} \neq \mathbf{0}$, then $\|\lambda\|^{2} \geq 4 d_{H}\left(C_{1}\right)$; finally, if $\boldsymbol{a}_{0}=\boldsymbol{a}_{1}=\mathbf{0}$, then $\|\lambda\|^{2} \geq 16$ (if $\lambda \neq \mathbf{0}$ ). This suggests that we shall want to choose codes $C_{1}$ and $C_{0}$ for which the Hamming distances are in the ratio 1:4. Most of the mod-4 binary lattices useful in practice are decomposable. For example, the lattice $R E_{8}$ is a version of the Gosset lattice which is mod-4 and decomposable, with the code formula $R E_{8}=4 \boldsymbol{Z}^{8}+2(8,7,2)+(8,1,8)$. The standard binary representation of an integer modulo 4 is a more elementary example of a decomposition of this type, with the code formula $\boldsymbol{Z}=\mathbf{4 Z}+2(1,1)+(1,1)$; this simply means that every integer modulo 4 can be expressed as a binary linear combination of the two generators 2 and 1 , where the combination can be specified by a 2-bit label. The simplest example of an indecomposable mod-4 lattice is the two-dimensional lattice $\Lambda=\left\{\left(\boldsymbol{x}_{1}, \boldsymbol{x}_{2}\right): \boldsymbol{x}_{1}+\boldsymbol{x}_{2} \equiv\right. 0 \bmod 4\}$, for which $C$ is the $(2,1)$ code with generator $(1,1)$, so $2 C$ has generator $\boldsymbol{g}_{1}=(2,2)$, but $\boldsymbol{g}_{2}$ must be taken as $(1,-1)$ or $(-1,1)$; however, this lattice can be made decomposable by inverting the sign of one coordinate. The Leech lattice $\Lambda_{24}$ is an example of a fundamentally indecomposable mod-4 lattice. Fig. 8(a) illustrates the coset decomposition $\Lambda / \Lambda_{e} / 4 \boldsymbol{Z}^{N}$ of a mod-4 binary lattice that is used above. The $K^{\prime}$-bit label $\boldsymbol{a}_{1}$ specifies a coset of $\boldsymbol{4} \boldsymbol{Z}^{N}$ in the partition $\Lambda_{e} / \boldsymbol{4} \boldsymbol{Z}^{N}$, and the ( $K-K^{\prime}$ )-bit label $\boldsymbol{a}_{0}$ specifies a coset of $\Lambda_{e}$ in the partition $\Lambda / \Lambda_{e}$. Altogether, therefore, the $K$-bit label ( $\boldsymbol{a}_{0}, \boldsymbol{a}_{1}$ ) specifies a coset of $\mathbf{4} \boldsymbol{Z}^{N}$ in the partition $\Lambda / \mathbf{4} \boldsymbol{Z}^{N}$. Fig. 8(b) illustrates the same decomposition when $\Lambda$ is a decomposable mod-4 binary lattice with code formula $\Lambda=4 \boldsymbol{Z}^{N}+2 C_{1}+C_{0}$; the form is then that of a coset code $\mathbb{C}\left(\boldsymbol{Z}^{N} / 2 \boldsymbol{Z}^{N} / 4 \boldsymbol{Z}^{N} ; C_{0}, C_{1}\right)$. (If $C_{1}$ is the ( $N, N$ ) code, then $\Lambda_{e}=2 \boldsymbol{Z}^{N}, \Lambda$ is actually a mod-2 lattice, and Fig. 8 reduces to Fig. 7.) ![](/documents/a12a64b5-5d83-44f3-b724-a58cf3053e87/images/image_008.jpg) Fig. 8. Illustration of mod-4 binary lattice $\Lambda$ as union of $2^{K}$ cosets of $\boldsymbol{4} \boldsymbol{Z}^{N}$, each coset corresponding to binary label ( $\boldsymbol{a}_{0}, \boldsymbol{a}_{1}$ ). (a) General case. (b) Case where $\Lambda$ is decomposable. It should be clear how to extend Fig. 8 to real binary lattices of any 2-depth $m$, using a partition chain $\Lambda / \Lambda_{e} / \cdots / 2^{m} Z^{N}$ of sublattices of $\Lambda$ consisting of all elements of $\Lambda$ whose coordinates are in $2 \boldsymbol{Z}^{N}, 4 \boldsymbol{Z}^{N}$, and so forth. The decomposition of complex binary lattices is similar. Mod-2 complex binary $\boldsymbol{G}$-lattices are always decomposable, with a code formula of the form $\Lambda=\phi^{2} \boldsymbol{G}^{N}+ \phi C_{1}+C_{0}$, as we can see from the following lemma. Complex binary lattices with depths of three or more are not necessarily decomposable, but the ones that we are concerned with generally are. Lemma 4: An $N$-dimensional complex $G$-lattice $\Lambda$ is a mod-2 binary lattice if and only if it is the set of all Gaussian integer $N$-tuples that are congruent modulo $\phi^{2}$ to an $N$-tuple of the form $\phi \boldsymbol{c}_{1}+\boldsymbol{c}_{0}$, where $\boldsymbol{c}_{1}$ is a codeword in a binary ( $N, K$ ) code $C_{1}$, and $c_{0}$ is a codeword in a binary ( $N, J-K$ ) code $C_{0}$ which is a subcode of $C_{1}$. The redundancy of $\Lambda$ is $r(\Lambda)=2 N-J$, and its minimum squared distance is $$ d_{\min }^{2}(\Lambda)=\min \left[4,2 d_{H}\left(C_{1}\right), d_{H}\left(C_{0}\right)\right] . $$ Proof: If $\Lambda$ is a mod-2 binary lattice, then $\boldsymbol{G}^{N} / \Lambda / 2 \boldsymbol{G}^{N}=\phi^{2} \boldsymbol{G}^{N}$ is a partition chain, and $\Lambda / 2 \boldsymbol{G}^{N}$ has order $2^{J}$ for some integer $J$. Since $\boldsymbol{G}^{N} / 2 \boldsymbol{G}^{N}$ has order $2^{2 N}$, $\boldsymbol{G}^{N} / \Lambda$ has order $2^{2 N-J}$, so the redundancy of $\Lambda$ is $r(\Lambda)= 2 N-J . \Lambda$ is the union of $2^{J}$ cosets of $2 \boldsymbol{G}^{N}=\phi^{2} \boldsymbol{G}^{N}$, and we may take the coordinates of the coset representative of any such coset to be of the form $\phi \boldsymbol{c}_{1}+\boldsymbol{c}_{0}$, where $\boldsymbol{c}_{1}$ and $\boldsymbol{c}_{0}$ are binary $N$-tuples. Sums of coset representatives may be taken $\bmod 2 G^{N}$ (or modulo 2 ). Consider the set $\Lambda_{\phi}$ of all points in $\Lambda$ whose coordinates are all multiples of $\phi$. Then $\Lambda / \Lambda_{\phi} / \phi^{2} G^{N}$ is a partition chain, and there is a coset decomposition of the form $\Lambda=\phi^{2} \boldsymbol{G}^{N}+\left[\Lambda_{\phi} / \phi^{2} \boldsymbol{G}^{N}\right]+\left[\Lambda / \Lambda_{\phi}\right]$. The generators of the lattice $\Lambda_{\phi}$ modulo $\phi^{2} \boldsymbol{G}^{N}$ may be taken as $\phi \boldsymbol{g}_{k}, 1 \leq k \leq K$, for some $K$, where each $\boldsymbol{g}_{k}$ is a binary $N$-tuple; consequently, $\Lambda_{\phi}=\phi^{2} G^{N}+\phi C_{1}$, where $C_{1}$ is the binary ( $N, K$ ) code generated by these $\boldsymbol{g}_{k}$. Similarly, the generators of the lattice $\Lambda$ modulo $\Lambda_{\phi}$ may be taken as $\boldsymbol{g}_{k}, K+1 \leq k \leq J$, where each $\boldsymbol{g}_{k}$ is a binary $N$-tuple; consequently, $\Lambda= \Lambda_{\phi}+C_{0}$, where $C_{0}$ is the binary ( $N, J-K$ ) code generated by the $\boldsymbol{g}_{k}, K+1 \leq k \leq J$. Since $\Lambda$ is a $\boldsymbol{G}$-lattice, $\lambda \in \Lambda$ implies $\phi \lambda \in \Lambda$. Therefore, $C_{0}$ must be a subcode of $C_{1}$. The minimum distance expression arises from the fact that there are $N$-tuples of norm 4 in $\phi^{2} G^{N}$, of norm $2 d_{H}\left(C_{1}\right)$ in $\phi C_{1}$, and of norm $d_{H}\left(C_{0}\right)$ in $C_{0}$; conversely, if $\lambda \equiv \phi c_{1}+c_{0} \bmod \phi^{2}$ and $c_{0} \neq \mathbf{0}$, then $\|\lambda\|^{2} \geq d_{H}\left(C_{0}\right)$; if $\boldsymbol{c}_{0}=\mathbf{0}$ but $\boldsymbol{c}_{1} \neq \mathbf{0}$, then $\|\lambda\|^{2} \geq 2 d_{H}\left(C_{1}\right)$; finally, if $\boldsymbol{c}_{0}=\boldsymbol{c}_{1} =\mathbf{0}$, then $\|\lambda\|^{2} \geq 4$ (if $\lambda \neq \mathbf{0}$ ). For the converse, let $\Lambda$ be the set of all Gaussian integer $N$-tuples $\lambda \equiv \phi \boldsymbol{c}_{1}+\boldsymbol{c}_{0} \bmod \phi^{2}$. To show that $\Lambda$ is a $\boldsymbol{G}$ lattice, we must show that if $\lambda_{1}, \lambda_{2} \in \Lambda$, then $\lambda_{1}+\lambda_{2} \in \Lambda$, and also if $\lambda \in \Lambda$, then $-\lambda \in \Lambda$ and $i \lambda \in \Lambda$. The first two propositions follow immediately from $2 \lambda \equiv 0 \bmod \phi^{2}$. The third follows from $i \lambda=\phi \lambda-\lambda \in \Lambda$, where the fact that $\phi \lambda \in \Lambda$ depends on $C_{0}$ being a subcode of $C_{1} . \Lambda$ is a Gaussian integer lattice with $2 G^{N}$ as a sublattice (by construction). A mod-2 complex $\boldsymbol{G}$-lattice $\Lambda$ has depth 1 if and only it the code $C_{1}$ of Lemma 4 is the ( $N, N$ ) code; then its code formula can be simplified to $\Lambda=\phi \boldsymbol{G}^{N}+C_{0}$. Otherwise, it has depth 2. For example, as a complex lattice, the mod-2 depth-1 Schläfli lattice $D_{4}$ is decomposable with the code formula $$ D_{4}=\phi \boldsymbol{G}^{2}+(2,1,2) . $$ Thus $\mu\left(D_{4}\right)=1, r\left(D_{4}\right)=1, \rho\left(D_{4}\right)=1 / 2$, and $d_{\text {min }}^{2}\left(D_{4}\right)=$ 2, in agreement with the values obtained earlier for $D_{4}$ as a real lattice. As a complex lattice, the mod-2 depth-2 Gosset lattice $E_{8}$ is decomposable with the code formula $$ E_{8}=\phi^{2} \boldsymbol{G}^{4}+\phi(4,3,2)+(4,1,4) $$ (see part II). Thus $\mu\left(E_{8}\right)=2, r\left(E_{8}\right)=4, \rho\left(E_{8}\right)=1$, and $d_{\text {min }}^{2}\left(E_{8}\right)=4$, in agreement with the values obtained earlier for $E_{8}$ as a real lattice. The complex binary representation of a Gaussian integer $\bmod \phi^{2}$ is a more elementary example of a decomposition of this type. As in the real case, complex binary lattices that are not mod-2 are not necessarily decomposable, i.e., expressible purely in terms of binary codes. However, the lattices that are useful in applications generally are as follows. Let $C_{\mu-1} / C_{\mu-2} / \cdots / C_{0}$ be a partition chain of binary ( $N, K_{j}$ ) codes, $0 \leq j \leq \mu-1$, i.e., $C_{j}$ is a subcode of $C_{j+1}$. Then let $\Lambda$ be a complex binary lattice whose elements are the set of Gaussian integer $N$-tuples $\lambda$ that are congruent to $\phi^{\mu-1} \boldsymbol{c}_{\mu-1}+\cdots+\boldsymbol{c}_{0}$ modulo $\phi^{\mu}$, where $\boldsymbol{c}_{j}$ is a codeword in the code $C_{j}$, i.e., the coefficients of $\phi^{j}$ in the complex binary representation of $\lambda$ are codewords in $C_{j}, 0 \leq j \leq \mu-1$. We represent this by the (complex) code formula $$ \Lambda=\phi^{\mu} \boldsymbol{G}^{N}+\phi^{\mu-1} C_{\mu-1}+\cdots+C_{0} . $$ This is the coordinate array idea again, but using the complex binary representation rather than the standard binary representation. Some of the principal properties of such a complex decomposable lattice are as follows. a) $\Lambda$ is a $\boldsymbol{G}$-lattice, because if $\lambda \in \Lambda$, then $\phi \lambda \in \Lambda$, in view of the subcode structure of the code formula; thus $i \lambda=(\phi-1) \lambda \in \Lambda$. b) $\Lambda$ has depth $\mu$ (assuming that $K_{\mu-1}t^{\prime}$; e.g., $\mathbb{C}_{1}$ is a proper subset of $\mathbb{C}_{0}$. Define two code sequences as equivalent modulo $\mathbb{C}_{1}$ if their first difference is at time $t$ or later. Two sequences in $\mathbb{C}_{0}$ are then equivalent modulo $\mathbb{C}_{1}$ if and only if their first element $c_{0}$ is the same. Let $\Lambda_{0}$ then be the set of all possible first elements $c_{0}$; that is, $\Lambda_{0}=\left\{\lambda: \lambda \in \Lambda^{\prime}+\right. \boldsymbol{c}\left(\boldsymbol{a}_{0}\right)$ \}, where $\boldsymbol{a}_{0}$ is a possible time-zero output from the encoder $C$ given that all previous outputs were zero or, equivalently, given that the encoder starts in the zero state. There are $2^{k}$ such $a_{0}$, and thus $\Lambda_{0}$ is the union of $2^{k}$ cosets of $\Lambda^{\prime}$. Ordinarily, $\Lambda_{0}$ is a lattice, which we call the time-zero lattice. By Lemma $1, V\left(\Lambda_{0}\right)=2^{-k} V\left(\Lambda^{\prime}\right)$; since $V\left(\Lambda^{\prime}\right)=2^{k+r} V(\Lambda)$, we also have $V\left(\Lambda_{0}\right)=2^{r} V(\Lambda)$. In an appropriate sense, therefore, the equivalence classes of $\mathbb{C}_{0}$ modulo $\mathbb{C}_{1}$, which we may write as $\mathbb{C}_{0} / \mathbb{C}_{1}$, are isomorphic to the time-zero lattice $\Lambda_{0}$. The set $\mathbb{C}_{0}$ has the decomposition $$ \mathbb{C}_{0}=\mathbb{C}_{0} / \mathbb{C}_{1}+\mathbb{C}_{1} / \mathbb{C}_{2}+\cdots $$ which by time-invariance is isomorphic to the Cartesian product $\Lambda_{0} \times \Lambda_{0} \times \cdots$. In other words, $\mathbb{C}_{0}$ fills space as densely as does $\Lambda_{0} \times \Lambda_{0} \times \cdots$. Thus it is reasonable to define the fundamental volume of $\mathbb{C}$ per $N$ dimensions as $V(\mathbb{C})=V\left(\Lambda_{0}\right)$. Now we may define the fundamental coding gain of a trellis code in the same way as we did for a lattice: $$ \gamma(\mathbb{C})=d_{\min }^{2}(\mathbb{C}) / V(\mathbb{C})^{2 / N} . $$ Let us now write $k(C)$ and $r(C)$ for the parameters $k$ and $r$ of a convolutional code $C$, and $\kappa(C)$ and $\rho(C)$ for the normalized informativity and redundancy $\kappa(C)= 2 k(C) / N$ and $\rho(C)=2 r(C) / N$, respectively. Since $V(\mathbb{C}) =V\left(\Lambda_{0}\right)=2^{-k(C)} V\left(\Lambda^{\prime}\right)=2^{r(C)} V(\Lambda)$, we also have the expressions $$ \begin{aligned} \gamma(\mathbb{C}) & =2^{\kappa(C)} d_{\min }^{2}(\mathbb{C}) / V\left(\Lambda^{\prime}\right)^{2 / N} \\ & =2^{\kappa(C)}\left[d_{\min }^{2}(\mathbb{C}) / d_{\min }^{2}\left(\Lambda^{\prime}\right)\right] \gamma\left(\Lambda^{\prime}\right) \\ & =2^{\kappa(\mathbb{C})}\left[d_{\min }^{2}(\mathbb{C}) / 2^{\mu(\mathbb{C})}\right] \end{aligned} $$ where $\kappa(\mathbb{C})=\kappa(C)+\kappa\left(\Lambda^{\prime}\right)$ and $\mu(\mathbb{C})=\mu\left(\Lambda^{\prime}\right)$. Also, $$ \begin{aligned} \gamma(\mathbb{C}) & =2^{-\rho(C)} d_{\min }^{2}(\mathbb{C}) / V(\Lambda)^{2 / N} \\ & =2^{-\rho(C)}\left[d_{\min }^{2}(\mathbb{C}) / d_{\min }^{2}(\Lambda)\right] \gamma(\Lambda) \\ & =2^{-\rho(\mathbb{C})} d_{\min }^{2}(\mathbb{C}) \end{aligned} $$ where $\rho(\mathbb{C})=\rho(C)+\rho(\Lambda)$. Thus if we define the normalized redundancy, informativity, and depth of the trellis code $\mathbb{C}$ as the sums of the corresponding quantities for the code $C$ and partition $\Lambda / \Lambda^{\prime}$, where we regard the depth of $C$ as 0 , then we get expressions analogous to those that we obtained for lattices. The following lemma is both useful in itself and also gives an intuitive explanation of these formulas. Lemma 5: If $\mathbb{C}\left(\Lambda / \Lambda^{\prime} ; C\right)$ is a trellis code based on a partition $\Lambda / \Lambda^{\prime}$ of binary lattices, where the depth of $\Lambda^{\prime}$ is $\mu$, and a $2^{\nu}$-state, rate- $k /(k+r)$ convolutional code $C$, then there is an equivalent trellis code $\mathbb{C}\left(\boldsymbol{G}^{N} / \phi^{\mu} \boldsymbol{G}^{N^{\prime}} ; C^{\prime}\right)$ based on the partition $\boldsymbol{G}^{N} / \phi^{\mu} \boldsymbol{G}^{N}$, where $C^{\prime}$ is a $2^{\nu}$-state, rate- $\left[k+k\left(\Lambda^{\prime}\right)\right] / N \mu$ convolutional code, and $N$ is the dimension of $\Lambda$ or $\Lambda^{\prime}$ as complex lattices. Proof: If $\Lambda^{\prime}$ is a binary lattice of depth $\mu$, then $\boldsymbol{G}^{N} / \Lambda / \Lambda^{\prime} / \phi^{\mu} \boldsymbol{G}^{N}$ is a partition chain, with $\left|\boldsymbol{G}^{N} / \Lambda\right|=2^{r(\Lambda)}$, $\left|\Lambda / \Lambda^{\prime}\right|=2^{k+r},\left|\Lambda^{\prime} / \phi^{\mu} \boldsymbol{G}^{N}\right|=2^{k\left(\Lambda^{\prime}\right)}$, and $\left|\boldsymbol{G}^{N} / \phi^{\mu} \boldsymbol{G}^{N}\right|=2^{N_{\mu}}$. In view of the coset decomposition $\boldsymbol{G}^{N}=\phi^{\mu} \boldsymbol{G}^{N}+ \left[\Lambda^{\prime} / \phi^{\mu} \boldsymbol{G}^{N}\right]+\left[\Lambda / \Lambda^{\prime}\right]+\left[\boldsymbol{G}^{N} / \Lambda\right]$, we may select a coset of $\phi^{\mu} \boldsymbol{G}^{\boldsymbol{N}}$ in the partition $\boldsymbol{G}^{\boldsymbol{N}} / \phi^{\mu} \boldsymbol{G}^{\boldsymbol{N}}$ by the following set of $N \mu$ bits: an all-zero $r(\Lambda)$-tuple $\mathbf{0}$, which selects the zero coset of $\Lambda$ in the partition $G^{N} / \Lambda$, namely, $\Lambda$ itself; a $(k+r)$-tuple $\boldsymbol{a}$, which selects the coset $\Lambda^{\prime}+\boldsymbol{c}(\boldsymbol{a})$ of $\Lambda^{\prime}$ in the partition $\Lambda / \Lambda^{\prime}$ as in the original trellis code $\mathbb{C}\left(\Lambda / \Lambda^{\prime} ; C\right)$; and finally, a $k\left(\Lambda^{\prime}\right)$-tuple $a^{\prime}$ of "uncoded bits" which selects one of the $2^{k\left(\Lambda^{\prime}\right)}$ cosets of $\phi^{\mu} G^{N}$ whose union is $\Lambda^{\prime}$. These $N \mu$ bits can be regarded as the outputs of an augmented convolutional encoder $C^{\prime}$, which has $k+k\left(\Lambda^{\prime}\right)$ information bits, $r+r(\Lambda)$ redundant bits, and the same number of states as the original encoder for $C$, as illustrated in Fig. 11. The set of code sequences that may be generated by this augmented encoder are the same as those in the original code $\mathbb{C}\left(\Lambda / \Lambda^{\prime} ; C\right)$. ![](/documents/a12a64b5-5d83-44f3-b724-a58cf3053e87/images/image_011.jpg) Fig. 11. Augmented encoder $C^{\prime}$ of Lemma 5. An alternative form of Lemma 5 is as follows. If the 2-depth of $\Lambda^{\prime}$ is $m$, then $\mathbb{C}\left(\Lambda / \Lambda^{\prime} ; C\right)$ is equivalent to a code $\mathbb{C}\left(\boldsymbol{Z}^{N} / 2^{m} \boldsymbol{Z}^{N^{\prime}} ; C^{\prime}\right)$ based on the partition $\boldsymbol{Z}^{N} / 2^{m} \boldsymbol{Z}^{N}$, where $C^{\prime}$ is a $2^{\nu}$-state, rate- $[N m-r-r(\Lambda)] / N m$ convolutional code, and $N$ is the dimension of $\Lambda$ or $\Lambda^{\prime}$ as real lattices. The proof is essentially the same. Indeed, if $\mu$ is even, $\mu=2 m$, then the partition $\boldsymbol{Z}^{2 N} / \Lambda / \Lambda^{\prime} / 2^{m} \boldsymbol{Z}^{2 N}$ is the same as $\boldsymbol{G}^{N} / \Lambda / \Lambda^{\prime} / \boldsymbol{\phi}^{\mu} \boldsymbol{G}^{N}$, and the augmented encoder $C^{\prime}$ is the same; if $\mu$ is odd, $\mu=2 m-1$, then the partition $\boldsymbol{Z}^{2 N} / \Lambda / \Lambda^{\prime} / 2^{m} \boldsymbol{Z}^{2 N}$ is an extension of $\boldsymbol{G}^{N} / \Lambda / \Lambda^{\prime} / \phi^{\mu} \boldsymbol{G}^{N}$ by the partition $\phi^{\mu} \boldsymbol{G}^{N} / \phi^{\mu+1} \boldsymbol{G}^{N}$, and the augmented encoder $C^{\prime}$ just uses $N$ more uncoded bits. The coding gain $\gamma(\mathbb{C})$ may now be related to that of the lattices $\boldsymbol{G}^{N}, \Lambda, \Lambda^{\prime}$, and $\phi^{\mu} \boldsymbol{G}^{N}$ as follows. Relative to $\gamma\left(\Lambda^{\prime}\right)=2^{\kappa\left(\Lambda^{\prime}\right)}\left[d_{\text {min }}^{2}\left(\Lambda^{\prime}\right) / 2^{\mu\left(\Lambda^{\prime}\right)}\right]$, the gain $\gamma(\mathbb{C})$ is greater by a factor of $2^{\kappa(C)}$ due to the fact that $\mathbb{C}$ conveys $\kappa(C)$ more bits of information per two dimensions, offset by a distance loss factor of $d_{\text {min }}^{2}(\mathbb{C}) / d_{\text {min }}^{2}\left(\Lambda^{\prime}\right)$ (if any). Relative to $\gamma(\Lambda)=2^{-\rho(\Lambda)} d_{\text {min }}^{2}\left(\Lambda^{\prime}\right)$, the gain $\gamma(\mathbb{C})$ is greater by the distance gain factor of $d_{\text {min }}^{2}(\mathbb{C}) / d_{\text {min }}^{2}(\Lambda)$, offset by a power loss of $2^{-\rho(C)}$ due to constellation expansion. If $\Lambda^{\prime}=\phi^{\mu} \boldsymbol{G}^{N}$, then $\gamma\left(\Lambda^{\prime}\right)=1$ and $d_{\text {min }}^{2}\left(\Lambda^{\prime}\right)=2^{\mu\left(\Lambda^{\prime}\right)}$, so $\gamma(\mathbb{C})$ is simply $2^{\kappa(\mathbb{C})}$, offset by a distance loss factor of $d_{\text {min }}^{2}(\mathbb{C}) / 2^{\mu\left(\Lambda^{\prime}\right)}$ (if any). If $\Lambda=\boldsymbol{G}^{N}$, then $\gamma(\Lambda)=1$ and $d_{\text {min }}^{2}(\Lambda)=1$, so $\gamma(\mathbb{C})$ is simply $d_{\text {min }}^{2}(\mathbb{C})$, offset by a power loss of $2^{-\rho(\mathbb{C})}$ due to constellation expansion. This last expression is the simplest and shows that we need to know only the minimum squared distance $d_{\text {min }}^{2}(\mathbb{C})$ and the normalized redundancy $\rho(\mathbb{C})$ to compute the fundamental coding gain $\gamma(\mathbb{C})=2^{-\rho(\mathbb{C})} d_{\text {min }}^{2}(\mathbb{C})$, where the normalized redundancy is simply the sum of the normalized redundancies of the code $C$ and the lattice $\Lambda$. If $\Lambda=\boldsymbol{G}^{N} \simeq \boldsymbol{Z}^{2 N}$, then it suffices to know the normalized redundancy $r / N$ of the code $C$. A small normalized redundancy $\rho(\mathbb{C})$ is thus desirable to both minimize constellation expansion and maximize coding gain for a given $d_{\text {min }}^{2}(\mathbb{C})$, as was recognized by Wei [11]. Lemma 5 shows that all trellis codes based on partitions of binary lattices are equivalent to trellis codes based on partitions $\boldsymbol{G}^{N} / \phi^{\mu} \boldsymbol{G}^{N}$, or, by extension, partitions $\boldsymbol{Z}^{N} / 2^{m} \boldsymbol{Z}^{N}$ of the integer lattice $\boldsymbol{Z}^{N}$, so that in principle only these kinds of partitions need to be considered to discover all binary trellis codes. In practice, consideration of more general partitions $\Lambda / \Lambda^{\prime}$ both facilitates the search for good codes and simplifies their encoding and decoding. ## C. Linear Trellis Codes In general, if $c_{1}$ and $c_{2}$ are two code sequences in a trellis code $\mathbb{C}$, it is not necessarily true that $\boldsymbol{c}_{1}+\boldsymbol{c}_{2}$ and $c_{1}-c_{2}$ are also code sequences. When this property does hold, we say that $\mathbb{C}$ is a linear trellis code. In this section we give some examples of linear trellis codes, including the important case where $\Lambda / \Lambda^{\prime}$ is a partition of mod-2 binary lattices. If a trellis code is linear, then it is a lattice, albeit an infinite-dimensional lattice. Because it is a time-invariant infinite-dimensional lattice, it is usually possible to define its parameters on a per-unit-time or per-two-dimensions basis, so that they are perfectly finite and analogous to the parameters of a finite-dimensional lattice, as we have already seen in the previous section. In this sense, linear trellis codes are to finite-dimensional lattices as convolutional codes are to block codes. (To extend this analogy, nonlinear trellis codes are a generalization of nonlattice finite-dimensional sphere packings.) A trivial example of a linear trellis code is the repeated use of a lattice code. If $\Lambda$ is any lattice, let $\Lambda^{\infty}$ be defined as the set of all sequences ( $\lambda_{t}, \lambda_{t+1}, \cdots$ ), where $\lambda_{t} \in \Lambda$, $j \geq t$. This may be regarded as a trellis code based on the "dummy partition" $\Lambda / \Lambda$ where the convolutional encoder $C$ disappears. The trellis associated with $\Lambda^{\infty}$ has one state for each time $t$, and the branch in each time interval is labeled by $\Lambda$. If $\mathbb{C}\left(\Lambda / \Lambda^{\prime} ; C\right)$ is a linear trellis code, it is a sublattice of $\Lambda^{\infty}$ and has $\left(\Lambda^{\prime}\right)^{\infty}$ as a sublattice, so $\Lambda^{\infty} / \mathbb{C} /\left(\Lambda^{\prime}\right)^{\infty}$ is a partition chain. If the partition $\Lambda / \Lambda^{\prime}$ has depth $\mu$, then $\left(\boldsymbol{G}^{N}\right)^{\infty} / \mathbb{C} /\left(\phi^{\mu} \boldsymbol{G}^{N}\right)^{\infty}$ is a partition chain; if it has 2 -depth $m$, then $\left(\boldsymbol{Z}^{N}\right)^{\infty} / \mathbb{C} /\left(2^{m} \boldsymbol{Z}^{N}\right)^{\infty}$ is a partition chain. A trellis code $\mathbb{C}$ has little chance of being linear unless the mapping $c(a)$ from encoder output ( $k+r$ )-tuples (labels) $\boldsymbol{a}$ to coset representatives $\boldsymbol{c}$ is linear modulo $\Lambda^{\prime}$, as in Lemma 2; i.e., $\boldsymbol{c}(\boldsymbol{a})=\boldsymbol{a} G$, where $G=\left\{\boldsymbol{g}_{j}, 1 \leq j \leq k+r\right\}$ is a generator matrix of $k+r$ vectors of $\Lambda$ that span $\Lambda$, modulo $\Lambda^{\prime}$. The following lemma shows that when $\Lambda / \Lambda^{\prime}$ is a partition of mod-2 lattices and the labeling map is linear, $\mathbb{C}$ is linear, and indeed isomorphic to a binary convolutional code, in the same sense as a mod-2 binary lattice $\Lambda$ is isomorphic to a binary block code, described in Lemma 3 (recall that if $\boldsymbol{c}(\boldsymbol{a})$ is an Ungerboeck labeling, it is linear modulo $\Lambda^{\prime}$ ). Lemma 6: If $\Lambda^{\prime}$ is a mod-2 lattice, $C$ is a $2^{\nu}$-state, rate- $k /(k+r)$ convolutional code and the labeling map $\boldsymbol{c}(\boldsymbol{a})$ is linear modulo $\Lambda^{\prime}$, then a trellis code $\mathbb{C}\left(\Lambda / \Lambda^{\prime} ; C\right)$ is the set of all sequences of integer $N$-tuples that are congruent modulo 2 to one of the words in a $2^{\nu}$-state rate-$[N-r(\mathbb{C})] / N$ convolutional code $C^{\prime}$. The redundancy of $\mathbb{C}$ is $r(\mathbb{C})=r+r(\Lambda)$, and its minimum squared distance is $d_{\text {min }}^{2}(\mathbb{C})=\min \left[4, d_{H}\left(C^{\prime}\right)\right]$. Sketch of proof: By the extension of Lemma 5, $\mathbb{C}$ is equivalent to a code based on the partition $\boldsymbol{Z}^{N} / 2 \boldsymbol{Z}^{N}$, where the augmented encoder $C^{\prime}$ has $N$ output bits and redundancy $r\left(C^{\prime}\right)=r+r(\Lambda)$. In the augmented encoder, the ( $k+r$ )-tuple $\boldsymbol{a}$ and the uncoded bits $\boldsymbol{a}^{\prime}$ specify a coset of $2 \boldsymbol{Z}^{N}$ in the partition $\boldsymbol{Z}^{N} / 2 \boldsymbol{Z}^{N}$, which may be specified by a binary $N$-tuple $\boldsymbol{c}^{\prime}\left(\boldsymbol{a}, \boldsymbol{a}^{\prime}\right)$. The mapping $\boldsymbol{c}^{\prime}\left(\boldsymbol{a}, \boldsymbol{a}^{\prime}\right)$ may be taken to be linear $\bmod 2$ if $\boldsymbol{c}(\boldsymbol{a})$ is linear modulo $\Lambda^{\prime}$. Thus $\mathbb{C}$ is set of all sequences of integer $N$-tuples that are congruent modulo 2 to one of the words in the convolutional code $C^{\prime}$. The minimum squared distance between code sequences corresponding to distinct codewords of $C^{\prime}$ is $d_{H}\left(C^{\prime}\right)$, and $d_{\min }^{2}\left(2 \boldsymbol{Z}^{N}\right)=4$. The four-state Ungerboeck code shown in Figs. 2, 3, and 10 is an example of a code of this type. The encoder of Fig. 10 is of the form of the augmented encoder of Fig. 11. Many of the important known codes to be listed in the next section, including Gallager-Calderbank-Sloane (GCS)-type codes and most of the Wei codes, are of this type. Even when a code is not linear, it may still be regular in the sense of Calderbank and Sloane [13]. A labeling $\boldsymbol{c}(\boldsymbol{a})$ is defined as regular if the minimum squared distance between points in two cosets $\Lambda^{\prime}+\boldsymbol{c}(\boldsymbol{a})$ and $\Lambda^{\prime}+\boldsymbol{c}\left(\boldsymbol{a}^{\prime}\right)$ is a function only of the mod-2 sum $\boldsymbol{a} \oplus \boldsymbol{a}^{\prime}$ of their labels or, equivalently, if the minimum norm in the coset $\Lambda^{\prime}+c(a) -\boldsymbol{c}\left(\boldsymbol{a}^{\prime}\right)$ is equal to the minimum norm in the coset $\Lambda^{\prime}+ \boldsymbol{c}\left(\boldsymbol{a} \oplus \boldsymbol{a}^{\prime}\right)$. For example, the 2-bit standard binary representation is a regular labeling of the four cosets in the partition $\boldsymbol{Z} / \mathbf{4 Z}$. If $\mathbb{C}\left(\Lambda / \Lambda^{\prime} ; C\right)$ is a code based on a partition $\Lambda / \Lambda^{\prime}$ with a regular labeling, the minimum squared distance between code sequences in the coset sequences $\boldsymbol{c}(\boldsymbol{a}(D))$ and $\boldsymbol{c}\left(\boldsymbol{a}^{\prime}(D)\right)$ corresponding to codewords $\boldsymbol{a}(D)$ and $\boldsymbol{a}^{\prime}(D)$ is then equal to the minimum norm of any code sequence in the coset sequence $c(a(D) \oplus \boldsymbol{a}^{\prime}(D)$ ) corresponding to the codeword $\boldsymbol{a}(D) \oplus \boldsymbol{a}^{\prime}(D)$. Therefore, the distribution of distances from any given code sequence to all other code sequences is the same as the norm distribution of code sequences, and the code is thus distance-invariant. A labeling is regular under any of the following conditions: a) if it is linear, in the sense that $\boldsymbol{c}(\boldsymbol{a})-\boldsymbol{c}\left(\boldsymbol{a}^{\prime}\right)=\boldsymbol{c}\left(\boldsymbol{a} \oplus \boldsymbol{a}^{\prime}\right)$ modulo $\Lambda^{\prime}$, e.g., whenever $\Lambda$ and $\Lambda^{\prime}$ are mod- 2 lattices; b) if it is an Ungerboeck labeling and the Ungerboeck distance bound always holds with equality, e.g., any Ungerboeck labeling for $\boldsymbol{Z}^{2} / R \boldsymbol{Z}^{2} / 2 \boldsymbol{Z}^{2} / 2 R \boldsymbol{Z}^{2} \simeq \boldsymbol{G} / \phi \boldsymbol{G} / \phi^{2} \boldsymbol{G} / \phi^{3} \boldsymbol{G}$; c) if $\Lambda$ and $\Lambda^{\prime}$ are $N$-fold Cartesian products $\Lambda^{N}$ and $\left(\Lambda^{\prime}\right)^{N}$, and the labeling for $\Lambda^{N} /\left(\Lambda^{\prime}\right)^{N}$ is the $N$-fold Cartesian product of a regular labeling for $\Lambda / \Lambda^{\prime}$, e.g., when the partition is $\boldsymbol{G}^{N} / \phi^{3} \boldsymbol{G}^{N}$ or $\boldsymbol{Z}^{N} / \mathbf{4} \boldsymbol{Z}^{N}$. In fact, regular labelings (although not necessarily regular Ungerboeck labelings) exist for all partitions used in all the codes covered in this paper. ## V. Known Classes of Trellis Codes We shall now categorize the principal classes of trellis codes that have so far appeared in the literature according to the parameters of the previous sections. In the next section, we give further generic classes. We shall then compare and contrast all of those schemes, including lattice codes. Ungerboeck [8] developed classes of one- and twodimensional trellis codes using rate- $k /(k+1)$ binary convolutional codes. From the viewpoint of this paper, his one-dimensional schemes are based on the four-way partition $\boldsymbol{Z} / \mathbf{4 Z}$ of the integers into the four residue classes modulo 4 , in combination with a binary rate- $1 / 2$ convolutional coder to select cosets of $\mathbf{4 Z}$. His two-dimensional schemes for rectangular constellations, which have the greatest practical importance, are based on either the fourway partition $\boldsymbol{Z}^{2} / 2 \boldsymbol{Z}^{2}$ in combination with a rate-1/2 convolutional encoder, or the eight-way partition $Z^{2} / 2 R Z^{2}$ with a rate- $2 / 3$ convolutional encoder. He also gives codes using phase-modulated constellations that are based on similar principles and may be regarded as coset codes (see Section I-C), but that will not be covered here. Table IV gives the characteristics of the Ungerboeck one- and two-dimensional schemes. The codes achieve increasing $d_{\text {min }}^{2}$ as the number $2^{\nu}$ of states increases from 4 to 512 , up to the maximum possible value of $d_{\text {min }}^{2}\left(\Lambda^{\prime}\right)=2^{\mu}$. (Note: We use the codes listed in [21], where minor corrections have been made in the earlier code tables.) The depth is $\mu=4$ for the one-dimensional schemes, while in two dimensions $\mu=2$ or 3 . The redundancy $r$ is one for both classes, but the normalized redundancy $\rho$ (per two dimensions) is thus two in the one-dimensional case, versus one in the two-dimensional case. The fundamental coding gain $\gamma$ is given by the formula $2^{-\rho} d_{\text {min }}^{2}$ and is also given in decibels. $N_{0}$ is the number of nearest neighbors, and $\tilde{N}_{0}=2 N_{0} / N$ is the error coefficient normalized to two dimensions. $N_{D}$ is the number of decoding operations using the trellis-based decoding algorithms of the partition $\Lambda / \Lambda^{\prime}$ whose complexity is given in Table III, followed by a conventional Viterbi algorithm for the convolutional code, and $\tilde{N}_{D}=2 N_{D} / N$ is the decoding complexity per two dimensions. (For each unit of time, for each of the $2^{\nu}$ states, the Viterbi algorithm requires $2^{k}$ additions and a comparison of $2^{k}$ numbers, or $2^{k}-1$ binary comparisons, so that its complexity is $\beta 2^{k+\nu}$, where $\beta=2-2^{-k}$, and $2^{k+\nu}$ is the number of branches per stage of the trellis, which is the measure of complexity used by Ungerboeck [21], following Wei [11].) The error coefficient reduces the effective coding gain by an amount that depends on the steepness of the error probability curve. In this paper, we will use the rule of thumb that every factor of two increase in the error coefficient reduces the coding gain by about 0.2 dB (at error rates of the order of $10^{-6}$ ); this will enable us to compute an effective coding gain $\gamma_{\text {eff }}$ (in dB), normalized for the error coefficient $\tilde{N}_{0}$. We will not go beyond the error coefficient $\tilde{N}_{0}$ in this paper, except for Ungerboeck-type codes, where we can present results of Eyuboglu and Li (unpublished) that take into account the next two normalized coefficients, $\tilde{N}_{1}$ and $\tilde{N}_{2}$. Honig [22] has performed a search for one-dimensional Ungerboeck-type codes based on the four-way partition $\boldsymbol{Z} / \mathbf{4} \boldsymbol{Z}$, using a criterion that includes the effect of the error coefficient, and has obtained an improvement at 64 states (the apparently improved 16 -state code is actually catastrophic). Similarly, Pottie and Taylor [23] have searched for two-dimensional Ungerboeck-type codes based on the eight-way partition $Z^{2} / 2 R Z^{2}$ and have obtained improvements at 64 and 128 states; the 128-state code has a lower fundamental coding gain $\gamma$ but a greater effective coding gain $\gamma_{\text {eff }}$ due to its much lower error coefficient. Finally, Eyuboglu and Li have made a reasonably exhaustive search for the best codes of both classes in terms of the effective coding gain criterion, for up to 256 states, with modest improvements at as few as 16 states. Fig. 12(a) plots the effective coding gain $\gamma_{\text {eff }}$ versus the normalized complexity $\tilde{N}_{D}$ for the best of these Unger-boeck-type one- and two-dimensional codes. We see that the graphs are fairly linear on this log-log plot over most of their range. An increase of a factor of two in complexity yields an increase of about 0.4 dB in coding gain, until the effective coding gain passes 5 dB . The one-dimensional codes are of the order of 0.2 dB better over this linear range (however, the two-dimensional codes have been generally preferred in practice because their constellation expansion factor $2^{\rho}$ is only two, not four). The first multidimensional code seems to have been developed by Gallager [1]. In this code, an eight-state rate3/4 convolutional encoder selects two successive cosets from the four-way two-dimensional partition $\boldsymbol{Z}^{2} / 2 \boldsymbol{Z}^{2}$ or, equivalently, one coset from the 16-way fourdimensional lattice partition $Z^{4} / 2 Z^{4}$ (equivalently, four successive cosets from the two-way one-dimensional partition $\boldsymbol{Z} / 2 \boldsymbol{Z}$ ). The basic idea, as in Lemma 6, is that with such partitions the minimum squared distance $d_{\text {min }}^{2}$ between code sequences is simply the minimum Hamming distance $d_{H}$ of the binary code, as long as $d_{H} \leq 4$. Quite independently, Calderbank and Sloane [10] discovered a very similar code, although with improved error coefficient $\tilde{N}_{0}$ due to the choice of an eight-state rate- $3 / 4$ binary code with a lower error coefficient.
